Xin Zhao fails when forced to engage without his charge available or when the target picked for isolation has stronger frontline peel than he can outrun, leaving him locked onto someone unkillable while ranged threats reposition outside his bubble.

name: Yasuo
rationale: Yasuo demands constant micro-positioning, chaining Sweeping Blade through minions and monsters to reposition while tracking Steel Tempest stack count so the third cast lands as a knockup on a priority target rather than a stray creep. Wind Wall timing is the highest-leverage decision in any fight, requiring projectile prediction against specific champion abilities, and a mistimed wall or a dash into the wrong unit collapses the entire engage. Skill transfers cleanly to other dash-resetting, animation-cancel-heavy champions and to anyone who rewards reading enemy cooldowns, since Yasuo's whole tempo runs on weaving autos between Q casts and saving Last Breath for self-initiated or ally-initiated airborne setups. The dominant failure mode is dashing into the enemy team without a follow-up knockup or flash available, leaving the wandering swordsman stranded once Flow shield breaks.

name: Yone
rationale: Yone executes through a layered dash economy where Q stacks toward a knockup, E commits a delayed return that demands the fight be won inside its window, and R functions as a long-range gap-close-and-reposition rather than a pure finisher. Decisions happen on nearly every basic attack cycle because shield uptime from Spectral Slash, Gathering Storm stack timing, and E-snapback positioning all overlap, so a Yone player is constantly choosing between extending for damage and rubber-banding out to safety. Skill carries over cleanly from other dash-and-weave melee patterns and from champions who chain mobility into a terminal crowd-control combo, particularly anyone used to managing a returnable resource like a recall window. The dominant failure mode is burning Q3 and E offensively, then having R cleansed, blocked, or wasted on a non-priority target, leaving Yone stranded in melee range with no exits and a shield that no longer refreshes fast enough to matter.

name: Yorick
rationale: Yorick plays as a layered setup loop where every trade begins with queuing Last Rites for the empowered swing, then converting the resulting grave into a Mist Walker pet that turns sustained pressure into a numbers game. Decisions happen at a slow, deliberate cadence: when to drop the wall to bisect a skirmish or cage a single target, when to spend Mourning Mist for the percent-health chunk and pet acceleration, and when to commit the Maiden as a siege anchor versus a teamfight pivot. Skill carries over from any champion that rewards spacing an extended-range autoattack and managing minion-like units, since Yorick's effective threat radius is wherever his ghouls and Maiden can reach rather than his own body. The failure mode is being caught without graves or summons in open terrain, where he lacks gap-close and burst, getting kited off his pets and reduced to a slow walker with a single empowered hit.

name: Yuumi
rationale: Yuumi spends most of the game attached to an anchor, which collapses her positioning decisions into anchor-selection and detach timing rather than personal movement, while her active inputs reduce to skillshot aiming on a guidable projectile and channel placement during the ultimate. The core decision loop is high-frequency but low-mechanical: choosing when to hop between allies based on incoming threats, when to land healing waves on clustered teammates, and when to peel mana and shields onto the carry carrying the fight. Skill transfer comes almost entirely from macro reading and target prioritization, since the projectile is the only meaningful aim check and the rest is cooldown sequencing layered onto a partner's playmaking. Yuumi fails when her anchor dies or gets caught out, leaving her with no untargetable frame, minimal solo damage, and no escape tools to survive isolation, so picking the wrong attachment in a teamfight cascades into losing the fight outright.

name: Zac
rationale: Zac's execution revolves around charging Elastic Slingshot from fog or brush to land a long-range engage that connects into Stretching Strikes' tether-attack and a max-health Unstable Matter detonation, with the player constantly weaving back through the fight to scoop blob chunks that simultaneously heal and shave the detonation's cooldown for repeat procs. Decisions cluster around when to hold the slingshot charge versus recast for the partial refund, and when to commit Let's Bounce as a follow-up disruption versus saving it to peel or reposition mid-bounce. Skill carries over from any windup-engage pattern where the player reads enemy positioning seconds before the actual collision, and the chunk-pickup loop rewards habitual movement discipline rather than mechanical execution. Zac fails when the slingshot is telegraphed and dodged, when health costs are spent without landing damage to generate chunks, or when the resurrection bloblets are cleaved down before they can recombine in a safe spot.

name: Zed
rationale: Zed plays around constant shadow placement, where every engagement requires pre-positioning a Living Shadow to create overlapping shuriken lanes and double Shadow Slash zones before committing Death Mark. The decision tempo is high and spatial—each cast demands simultaneous tracking of shadow position, return timing, and the mark's damage-storage window, with all-in burst sequences executed in under two seconds. Skill transfer is steep because the core mechanic of weaving shadow swaps with skillshots and auto-resets generalizes poorly from simpler kits, and learning Zed teaches reusable habits around projectile prediction and re-positioning mid-combo. The dominant failure mode is committing Death Mark without a shadow held in reserve, leaving no escape route after the dash and converting a kill attempt into a free pickup for the opponent.

name: Zeri
rationale: Zeri demands continuous micro-decisions every fraction of a second, alternating between Burst Fire spam for damage and charged basic attacks for poke and waveclear, while weaving Spark Surge through terrain to reposition and reset its cooldown via ability hits. Mastery hinges on understanding which attack type triggers what — knowing Burst Fire crits and applies on-hit only to the first target, while charged shots scale as spell damage and percent max health, fundamentally reshaping itemization and trade patterns. Ultrashock Laser placement against walls for guaranteed crits, combined with Lightning Crash timing to enter and sustain Overcharged, separates competent play from execution that snowballs teamfights. Skill from kited, terrain-heavy hyperscalers transfers reasonably well to Zeri, though players coming from standard auto-attack patterns often misuse her charged shot windows and waste Spark Surge resets. The dominant failure mode is mashing Burst Fire into single targets without managing charge timing or terrain angles, leaving Zeri immobile and underdamaging despite full resources.

name: Ziggs
rationale: Ziggs operates on continuous mid-range zone projection, lobbing Bouncing Bomb at predicted standing positions while interleaving auto attacks empowered by Short Fuse, which means every ability cast feeds back into the next empowered hit and the decision loop runs every couple of seconds rather than in discrete burst windows. Skilled play hinges on reading bounce arcs and minefield placement to deny terrain corridors, while Satchel Charge serves dual purposes as a self-peel displacement, a forced reposition on a key target, and a mobility tool through walls, requiring the player to commit a charge with intent rather than reflex. The signature execution of Ziggs is layering Hexplosive Minefield onto a Satchel knockback or onto a fleeing path so opponents eat compounded chip, then closing the sequence with Mega Inferno Bomb aimed at the exact retreat pixel rather than the current position. Skill transfers cleanly to any champion whose damage comes from arced, delayed-detonation skillshots where prediction and zoning matter more than aim-at-target accuracy. The failure mode is predictable backline lobbing without threat layering, where bombs land on empty ground, the satchel gets burned for movement rather than impact, and the ultimate is fired at where enemies were instead of where they must walk.

name: Zilean
rationale: Execution revolves around double-bombing a target by queuing the explosive, immediately resetting its cooldown, and stacking a second charge so both detonate together for burst, which makes the core combo a tight three-button sequence whose timing rarely changes between matchups. The harder decisions sit on the utility layer: who receives the speed buff during rotations, when to slow a diving threat, and most critically which ally gets the revive ult and at what health threshold, since casting it a moment too early wastes the window and a moment too late lets the target die outright. Zilean's macro layer adds a constant background calculation around the experience share, requiring lane-leaving timing and side-selection choices that compound over the early game. Skills transfer cleanly from anyone used to predictive shielding or revive timing, since the bomb combo itself is mechanically simple and the real difficulty is reading incoming damage. Failure for a Zilean player typically looks like burning the revive on someone already dead, mistiming the cooldown reset so only one bomb lands, or hoarding experience without ever transferring it to an ally who needed the level spike.

name: Zoe
rationale: Zoe rewards players who can chain ability casts into empowered autos while simultaneously aligning long-range Paddle Star trajectories through fog or terrain gaps, demanding constant prediction of where enemies will be one to two seconds in the future rather than where they currently stand. Every engagement involves layered decisions made within a fraction of a second: whether to Portal Jump for an angle on a max-range bubble, whether to recast the lingering star, and which summoner or active to weaponize through Spell Thief. Skill carries over from any champion built around skillshot prediction and animation cancels, but Zoe specifically demands comfort with self-displacement risk and with reading enemy flash and dash cooldowns to land sleep setups. The failure mode is harsh and immediate—missed bubbles leave her with no reliable escape, Portal Jump's return can drop her into collapsing enemies, and without a landed crowd control her damage windows simply do not open.

name: Zyra
rationale: Zyra's execution revolves around pre-planting seeds in expected engagement paths, then converting them into Thorn Spitters or Vine Lashers by layering Deadly Spines or Grasping Roots over them in the same cast window, which demands constant low-level decisions about seed placement between trades rather than burst moments of mechanical intensity. The hardest sequencing comes from landing a skillshot root through fog or brush onto a seed to spawn a Vine Lasher mid-engage, then dropping Stranglethorns to extend the knockup window while plants free-fire on the lifted target. Players who already think in terms of zone denial, projectile prediction, and stacked crowd control transfer in cleanly, since Zyra rewards the same anticipatory placement habits seen in other vine-and-skillshot toolkits. The dominant failure mode is wasting seed charges on reactive panic casts so that when a real engage arrives there is no plant army to convert, leaving only raw skillshot damage with no field pressure behind it.
